Our Team

Computational Chemistry & Molecular Modelling Lab

Department of Chemistry & Biochemistry  ·  Thapar Institute of Engineering & Technology, Patiala

We investigate the novel insights behind chemical driving forces, reaction mechanisms, and drug design — combining quantum mechanics, molecular simulation, and machine learning to tackle problems at the frontier of chemistry and biology.

Principal Investigator

Lab Director

Principal Investigator

Dr. Debashish Mandal

Associate Professor  ·  Department of Chemistry & Biochemistry  ·  Thapar Institute of Engineering & Technology
🎓 PhD, IACS Kolkata (2009–2013) 🔬 Post-doc, Hebrew University of Jerusalem (2013–2017) 📊 H-Index: 28 🆔 ORCID: 0000-0002-9452-6729
Density Functional Theory Machine Learning Molecular Docking Molecular Dynamics QM/MM Quantum Tunneling C–H Activation
⭐ Ranked in the World's Top 2% Scientists by Elsevier / Stanford University

Dr. Debashish Mandal leads the Computational Chemistry & Molecular Modelling (CCMM) Lab at Thapar University. His research uncovers the quantum mechanical driving forces behind chemical reactivity — with a particular focus on tunneling-controlled C–H activation, the influence of oriented external electric fields on catalysis, and structure-based drug development. Using Density Functional Theory (DFT), he investigates the electronic structure and spin states of high-valent metal–oxo and transition metal complexes, revealing how subtle changes in ligand architecture dramatically alter oxidative reactivity. His group further integrates Machine Learning to accelerate property prediction across large chemical datasets, and employs Molecular Docking alongside QM/MM simulations to bridge quantum-level insights with biological targets — enabling the rational design of therapeutically relevant compounds. This research is supported by a DST INSPIRE Faculty Grant and has resulted in publications across top-tier journals including Dalton Transactions, Inorganic Chemistry, Biomacromolecules, and Journal of Organic Chemistry.

📚 Google Scholar 🔬 ResearchGate 🆔 ORCID 🏛️ Thapar Profile ✉️ Email
Current Members

PhD Scholars

Graduate researchers actively pursuing their doctoral work across quantum chemistry, molecular simulation, and machine learning at CCMM Lab.

PhD Scholar
Amanpreet Kaur
Amanpreet's research focuses on computational drug discovery, where she develops Machine Learning and QSAR models to link molecular structure with drug–target interactions. She uses Molecular Docking and virtual screening to identify potential inhibitors against specific biological targets, and runs Molecular Dynamics simulations to examine the stability and behaviour of ligand–protein complexes. Her work also extends to mutation analysis, exploring how specific mutations influence drug efficacy — bringing together data-driven and structure-based approaches to support rational drug design.
Machine Learning Molecular Docking MD Simulations Mutation Analysis
✉️ Email 📚 Scholar 🆔 ORCID
JRF · PhD Scholar
Shubham Jain
Shubham is an M.S. Pharm. graduate in Medicinal Chemistry from NIPER Ahmedabad and currently serves as a JRF–PhD researcher in the DBT-sponsored Computational Chemistry Lab at Thapar Institute of Engineering & Technology. His research focuses on enzyme engineering, computational biocatalysis, and structure-based drug discovery. He employs Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations, Molecular Docking, QM/MM, DFT, and computational mutagenesis to investigate enzyme mechanisms, protein–ligand interactions, and rational protein design, with a current emphasis on engineering ω-transaminases for enhanced catalytic performance. Outside the lab, he enjoys swimming, travelling, and exploring new places.
MD Molecular Docking QM/MM DFT Enzyme Engineering
✉️ Email 💼 LinkedIn 🆔 ORCID
JRF · PhD Scholar
Priyanka
Priyanka holds an MSc in Medicinal Chemistry from Central University of Punjab and qualified CSIR-NET with AIR 44. Her current research focuses on the computational investigation of C–H bond activation and metal–oxo complexes using Density Functional Theory. Metal–oxo species are highly reactive intermediates central to oxidation catalysis, and her DFT studies probe their electronic structure, spin states, and reaction mechanisms at the quantum level. By mapping out potential energy surfaces and locating key transition states, her work provides atomic-scale insight into how these reactive species form and function in both synthetic and enzymatic contexts. Her background in medicinal chemistry complements her computational expertise, positioning her at the interface of catalyst design and mechanistic understanding.
DFT Metal–Oxo C–H Activation
✉️ Email 💼 LinkedIn
PhD Scholar
Vishvendra Raj
Vishvendra applies DFT to investigate how the metal center identity, oxidation state, and surrounding ligand environment collectively govern the reactivity of metal–oxo complexes. His calculations explore potential energy surfaces and electronic properties of these high-valent species, contributing to a deeper understanding of catalytic oxidation pathways. His work aims to provide theoretical guidance for the rational design of more efficient and selective metal-based catalysts for C–H activation.
DFT Metal–Oxo C–H Activation
✉️ Email
PhD Scholar
Timir Naskar
Timir takes a comprehensive multi-method approach to chemical research. He uses DFT to understand electronic structure and reaction energetics, Molecular Dynamics to study conformational dynamics and thermodynamic properties, and AI/ML techniques to build predictive models from quantum chemical data. His work also incorporates Molecular Docking to investigate binding interactions with biological targets — making him well-positioned to tackle problems that span both fundamental quantum chemistry and applied molecular recognition.
DFT MD AI/ML Docking
✉️ Email 📚 Scholar 🆔 ORCID
PhD Scholar
Harshita
Harshita's research centers on the quantum chemical characterization of metal–oxo complexes through DFT, with an emphasis on how spin state, electronic configuration, and ligand architecture influence oxidative reactivity. By performing detailed electronic structure analyses and mechanistic studies, her work bridges theoretical predictions with experimental observations — shedding light on how metal–oxo intermediates carry out challenging chemical transformations in synthetic and biological catalysis.
DFT Metal–Oxo Electronic Structure

Dissertation & Internship

Master's students contributing to the lab's research through dissertation projects and short-term internships.

Dissertation

M.Sc. Dissertation Student
Saaid Raina
Saaid is currently pursuing his M.Sc. in Chemistry at Thapar Institute of Engineering & Technology. For his dissertation, he is studying DNA Gyrase B as a potential antibacterial drug target, using a combination of Machine Learning, Molecular Docking, and Pharmacophore Modelling to identify and evaluate promising inhibitor candidates. His project reflects the lab's growing emphasis on integrating data-driven and structure-based approaches to accelerate early-stage drug discovery.
Machine Learning Molecular Docking Pharmacophore Modelling

Research Focus

Core Areas

Three interconnected themes that define the lab's scientific identity.

01

Tunneling-Controlled C–H Activation

Investigating how quantum mechanical tunneling governs hydrogen atom transfer in C–H bond cleavage, particularly in Fe(IV)–oxo and related high-valent transition metal systems. Tunneling probability, spin-inversion, and kinetic isotope effects are central to this work.

02

Oriented External Electric Fields in Catalysis

Exploring how externally applied electric fields can be used as a "reagent" to tune reaction barriers, shift transition states, and steer chemical selectivity — a frontier area with implications for electrocatalysis and molecular machines.

03

Drug Design & Biomolecular Simulation

Using Molecular Docking, QM/MM, and MD simulations to investigate enzyme mechanisms and computationally design bioactive molecules, bridging quantum-level chemical insight with therapeutically relevant biological targets.

Interested in joining the CCMM Lab?

We welcome motivated PhD applicants and project students with backgrounds in chemistry, physics, or computer science. Positions in computational chemistry, ML for chemistry, and molecular simulation are available. Funding through DST INSPIRE and other schemes.
